speaker: interviewer
text: Moses Essig. 12, 1995. Interviewer, Leah Zazulyer. Rochester, New York, USA, English language. Moses, before we talk more about Schindler, you said there were things about Plaszow that you still had in your mind. Do you want to talk about that a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow was like a cemetery. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That took the people, not just the Polish people, the Jewish people. They bring people from Europe and the French people. They bring over here, Plaszow. They shoot them over there. Yeah, international. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah. They put them by the walls, clean up, call them up, and that's all. Plaszow, that's-- this Plaszow was the same like today. Look what's happening around everywhere. You've got in Africa what's going on here. The same like in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened there that you most remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when I was supposed to go, when they brought a couple of people to shoot them. They killed them. I was, myself, in the grave to kill. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They took, the SS, took it. Come on. They tell, where you going? You go right over there with everybody. Sure. There'd be a lot of-- young people, old people. With them, it's nothing. The SS not, knew not. They go and guns. Most of them, you know, who helped the Germans, the Ukraine, Ukraine people. Oh, they was happy to shoot a Jew, oh! They put on all of their new uniforms. Yeah. They put on uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were lined up to be shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, once I was, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here come another German, tell them get out from over here. That's not for you. You need to go work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you scared?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. You see, they're not-- me, my brother, both of us was by the wall. Sure. Come another SS man, tell him, get out. What are you doing over here, both of you? Out! He hollered to us, get out! You have to do nothing. It's not your business to stay over here. Sure. It's even for the Nazis, the [INAUDIBLE] Nazi. They have to go with them because they're the Germany, the German. If they got another, they're going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think you were going to be shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you were thinking?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was already-- they tell me come on. We've got work for you. All right. Me and my brother, both of us, we go together, not separated. We sat over there on the side, me and my brother by the wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Comes the fuhrer from the German army tell them-- in German-- what are you doing over here? Tell them to send me over here. Who? Them? Out! He take out the back wall back to the camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's a big story to take about them. So is that all there is to that story?

speaker: interviewee
text: And after this, Schindler's coming. He come over there to the Plaszow. He talk with the lagerfuhrer, Goeth. That Goeth was terrible. Oh boy, oh, boy, that guy was.

speaker: interviewer
text: They shoot him. They kill him. When he come to us, to Brunnlitz, Schindler told them what are you looking over here? What do you think, you're important? Go tell them, out! Out from here. Picture after the end of war, they look after him. The SS, themselves, they look after him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was a big steal. They steal everything from the Jewish people. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I wish never come again. No, no, I don't wish that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got on the train, you went to Gross Rosen, and then, after a while, like how many days or nights--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, one night.

speaker: interviewer
text: One night, you got to--

speaker: interviewee
text: --sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, my brother, together, go to Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They tell us where to go. Mr. Schindler, himself, go is go with the people. He go with some in the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like the picture. Nothing changed. Let me tell you this. Mr. Schindler-- may he rest in peace-- he not go because of the Jews. [LAUGHS] He go for something else. You know, the Jewish people got the real one. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think he was doing it for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. And I can't tell you because I don't remember him. I remember when the war time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he walked through the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he walked through the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he say to people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. He not say nothing, no, no. He talk a little, and after this, he's go, take the car. He go home. I know of his wife too. His wife is still alive. She's in Argentina. I remember her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you think he walked through the train and then left? And the train went on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the train was gone. And he after that, after the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got to Brunnlitz, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We get off on the train. We go to make a ready for the beds. Then we go  and eat. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the food different from Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow, we never got food. Over here, we got food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of soup [INAUDIBLE]? Why would they cook over there? That's a long time, 50 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: More to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, eat. Beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you sleep, or how did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh? In bed. We got beds, special beds.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was that different from Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow was difference. Plaszow you got to sleep on the floor, in a bed, a different kind of bed, with wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Brunnlitz, what kind of bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Brunnlitz, what kind of bed was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: A different kind of bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Softer?

speaker: interviewee
text: [POLISH]. Go sit one, the bottom, another on the top. Like the army, army beds. No he-- what he was before, I don't know. And I can't tell you about that. He was a good man. He saved a lot of people. He saved 3,000 people. 3,000 people, not two or three. What he was before, I don't know. And I can't tell you because I don't know him. I know what I see in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you for work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ammunition. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know what ammunition is?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bombs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ammunition.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, they made those in Plaszow. Did they make them in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no they not make in Plaszow. They make over here, Brunnlitz. They make the bomb.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they made dishes too, didn't they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dishes, yeah, dishes. I work by dishes too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. What did you do in the making of dishes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I put it in the-- when they were about ready, put in the oven. And like this, like they make over here. When it was ready, I take them out. I put it in, on that side and that's all. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was never happy like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was a typical day like when you were working at Brunnlitz? You got up at what time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Brunnlitz, we got up seven o'clock, sometimes six o'clock. That's all. And I go ate breakfast. And after this, you go to the machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you eat in a cafeteria, a hall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, the cafeteria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or in the barracks? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --no, no, in the barracks. They got the cafeteria over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody ate where they slept?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they have role calls?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just went to work then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. See, but then sometimes was going outside to work. And after this, they come, somebody from the Germans tell them, hey, not let them out. And they ask, what's he doing? Because, as you see, there was care, when the Czechoslovakian come pick at us up and then take away in front of the Germans. They tell you, no more. No more over outside. No more. And then I go no more. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grapes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The grapes from the hills. The sun. They got the sun over there. Oh, a big one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the time you were working there, did you ever see Schindler again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. I didn't see Schindler. Schindler got another business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were people there [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] religious? Did they try to pray sometimes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. You see, where we come over there, the SS talked to the Czechoslovakian people. No go in. They ask them, why? Bandits coming over here. We are bandits. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the people that were working there, did they always--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, they work. The Czechoslovakian people, they work together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the people who were working there always make things the right way, or did they sometimes make things so they wouldn't work for the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- it's up to the people. If they want to work, they go right. They not right too. They don't care. They look out for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't know about any sabotage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No sabotage, no. They didn't make a sabotage over there. They are scared themselves. Even the German themselves are scared. A lot of Germans go away. They want to be over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brunnlitz? About four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it different at the end from the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was-- all of it was the same-- the same. He saved our lives. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He saved about 5,000 people, international. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He saved people from other places, and other camps and brought them over here to give them to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: He say that, you know what? No eat everything. I've got more people to come over here. OK, bring them. Sure, he give everybody-- even he was an SS man. And that was day, he give for everybody. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When I was in Israel, I was by his grave. What's it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: What's it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's a big stone. A big grave. He was a tall man. See, the Jewish make it for him. They took him to Israel. They respect him over there. I respect him too. I gave respect for him. He was a savior for 3,000 Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some people, no. How? How? Never mind. Look for somebody else like that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He save a couple kids. He took them where they kept us. I ask them, what are you doing over here? Somebody saved me. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even he was a Gestapo, SS. He no save for the Jews. He save for himself. No, he was a good man. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he worked too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he survived also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He died not about three, four years ago in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you hear anything about the rest of your family while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They can get at nothing. You in the camp, all you can do find out where they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did you find out what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I come finished, after the war. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were saying that you saw Schindler cry when he gave his speech and had to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no, I not see him. The people tell me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, tell me about what you hear.

speaker: interviewee
text: He cry-- they told me, he cry. He said goodbye. In a way, [INAUDIBLE]. You see, before the Russian come over, about two weeks ago, before the Russian supposed to come to Brunnlitz to take over. They're telling the people, hey, make a party, something for you. Get out because if they catch you, they'll kill you. Well, they collect their money, something to make something to give him a present. And he fly away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the present?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I no see it. I no was over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think he cried?

speaker: interviewee
text: To say goodbye. Why was-- why? Because he wants to be with the people. Even though he was a Gestapo, an SS man, he liked be the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who told you about this speech?

speaker: interviewee
text: A friend of mine. Yeah, he told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he say when he told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told him where's he? Oh, he's not more over here. See, but two weeks before he left, the Russians come over here. And if the Russians catch him, they would have killed him. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Russians liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians come over here. The Nazis come over, they opened up the doors. You want to go home? I told them, how we can go home? No we try train for you. I take the train. And two days later, you go out back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they feed you anything, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian like I gave them. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So how soon after they came did they put you on a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: About three or four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you happy to see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me tell you, some. First, all the kind Russian, they're good, they're bad. Back over here. They tell me, go home. Go, go-- when they come time-- go home. Go home. All right, we go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got on another train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, and my brother, we go on the train. Go ahead, back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Krakow? How long did that take?

speaker: interviewee
text: It takes a couple days, three, four days. See, from Czechoslovakia, they have to go to someplace else, change the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think you'd find when you got to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I try, maybe, I'll find somebody. And then I was going to my hometown, my house, my house where I was living. And they come out, hey, Moses, Moses, Moses. What's the matter?

speaker: interviewer
text: You know where your sister is? Where? Oh, OK, go see her. I catch one sister. After this, comes another sister, the brothers. That's all. Catch all together. Well, who told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The woman over there. She lived in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Someplace. They was working already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live when you got back to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I live over there in a hotel. In a hotel. I was living over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who paid the rent?

speaker: interviewee
text: No rent. You didn't pay rent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did-- who paid for it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The government pay for that. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they give you any other help?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We go to the Jewish organization. We get from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they give you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Money, sometimes to eat something. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your plan was then to stay in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your plan was to stay in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me tell you this. When I got family in Krakow, I will stay. When I come over to my family, let's all go to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: The sisters also and brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sisters, yeah. One sister was in a Nazi camp too, the younger sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: But were there any sisters still in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Brothers when you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not-- one sister was in Krakow when I was coming, yeah. She was working. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did she live?

speaker: interviewee
text: She worked with somebody. She would live over there where she working.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were going to stay in Krakow as long as she was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure we were. We have to wait. And after this, we go someplace, I get married in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did you get to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go back for organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: What organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish organization. I supposed to go to-- here I want to go this time to Israel. And I would belong to the organization. They tell me, OK, you want to go? Sure. It gave me to Germany. In Germany, I find my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in Germany did they send you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leipheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it? A camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a camp, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a displaced persons' camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about a year or two.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went from Brunnlitz back to Krakow, and then from Krakow to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister stayed in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the brother that was with you in Brunnlitz, what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was get married. He married. He got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you wanted to go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm go away. I'm go away.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to go to Israel. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you went to the camp in Germany. And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I just look at other people going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did you sleep all day or did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I never sleep. When I go someplace, never sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. Walk around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walk around, find friends. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's where you found your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was she from?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's from-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] she far from east-- I call them. I forget already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Galicia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Ah, what's the difference?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, anyway, somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, somewhere, and you found her.

speaker: interviewee
text: I found her in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I met her in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- now, you were telling me the first time you saw her, tell me about it again.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first time when I saw her I talked to my friend, you see the women that goes over there with the steps? Yeah. She be my wife. Oh, yeah. A couple days later, I told her come on. We get married. Yeah, sure. I told her, you alone. I'm alone. Both of us get together. OK. And we get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what made you decide, just looking at her, that that was the girl for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just look her in the face, thought, she's a good person. She was a good person. Yeah. She was a good person. I got her over here, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll look at the photographs later. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how did you get married in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: By a rabbi. He come over, the rabbi, and I get married. I think I got the marriage too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we'll look later, Moses.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so fast. Two or three days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. A couple days. Lets go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was agreeable to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. She told me, she's alone. She lose the whole family. Yeah, and I'm alone too. We go together. That's how we get like that. We get together and married. I live with her about 40 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so how long did you live in the camp before you left the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, about two years. And then I go back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and she went back to Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. And what did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go to work. Catch a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I can get at, I catched.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do? Clean or bake or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no, we first when we come, I work Gerber's baby food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, in this country.

speaker: interviewee
text: This country, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow, no. I get-- when I married her, I go back to Germany. And after Germany, I come over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you didn't go back to Krakow after you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. I married from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And then from Germany you came-- did you go to Israel or to United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, with Israel a difference [INAUDIBLE]. You see, Israel this time was in war. And I want to go Israel. Well, I got there. Israel told me, no, you no can go. All right. Because my wife got the boy, the son.

speaker: interviewer
text: I tell them, what I can do? I don't want to be over here. Well, make application to the American's Council down at the Israeli council. Israeli Council told me, you want in? You ready. I came in and 10 minutes you got the passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife told me no, not yet. I go to the American Council. American Council told them, no. I can not give you because you are a communist. I'm a communist. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The why me, a communist? I get up in the Nazi camp, and I'm a communist? No, and I can't go. I go by, Israeli called me come on. I give you the passport. So, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, what's happened, I have to wait another year. And after this, they give me, they call me up. Come on, I give you the passport--

words.text: they give me, they call me

speaker: interviewer
text: --for the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they think you were a communist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me. A lot of people like that were like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And your wife had a son from a first marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her husband had died?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. That, I was the husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your son too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my son. Not that-- she never got married before, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was born in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Germany, I go to Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. Into another camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- no, no. In Italy, I live in a private house.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK and it was from Italy that you went to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what made you go from Germany to Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I like it better from Germany go to Italy. And Italy it was better to go over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm-hm. Were there organizations helping you in Italy too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like, what organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish organization. I forget the name. It's a long time, about 50 years. See I was supposed to come over here before. And the United States-- Mr. [INAUDIBLE] didn't want to give me that part.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me I'm a communist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop.
